sentences.text: It's a good advantage, because I know where to put my hands.
This way, I didn't know to do the right or the middle.
This way, [LAUGHS] the right place for my hands.
Herman Schlesinger.
H-E-R-M-A-N, Schlesinger is S-C-H-L-E-S-I-N-G-E-R.
Yes.
And they me, here in America, Harry Schlesinger.
I was born in Czechoslovakia, 1929, March the 9th.
And the town is Mukacevo, in Czechoslovakia.
They used to call it Mukacevo.
When the Hungarians, they occupied it, it became Munkacs.
And in Jewish, they called it Munkatsh.
Today, I'm a 67-year-old.
Before the time I was born or during the time that I start to go to school?
So what would you like to know?
So because I'm a twin, and we will talk about the twin stories, what happened in concentration camp, so I'll start from the day I was born, 1929.
I am a twin with my sister.
And we were born in Munkatsh.
And--
My sister is half an hour older.
That's how-- I have to give her always respect.
And her name is Sitonia Gluck.
My father should rest in peace.
His name was Bentzion Schlesinger.
And my mother's name is Fany Schlesinger.
And in Israel, they used to call it [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [?
Feige ?] Schlesinger.
My father had a jewelry store in Munkatsh, a very nice place.
They called it, main street.
In Hungarian they called it [HUNGARIAN].
And we made a nice living, a comfortable, beautiful, nice living in Munkatsh.
I was not with my father in business, because, when I left Munkatsh to concentration camp, I was only close to 15 years old.
So I was a student.
And we had a good relationship.
When I was small, we used to go together to the shul.
We used to like play ball.
We used to like, together, skating.
And he liked the outside activities.
We had beautiful gardens behind the house.
And I used to help him, gardening.
And he liked all kind of hobbies.
My mother should rest in peace, a wonderful, Jewish mother, who liked always take care on her children.
She helped my father, a lot, in the store, in jewelry store, especially time Hitler gave an order that Jews has to go to forced labor.
And she took over the store.
From 1940 to 1944, she was managing all the years, the store.
We had very few workers.
There was two watchmakers and two jewelers.
And she took care of it, on the family and the store, and the same time, spent-- sending packages to my father, always to this forced labor.
Yeah, we have a religious background, from both sides, from my mother's side and from my father's side.
Munkatsh, itself, was a population for 30,000 people.
From this 30,000 people, there are 15,000 Jews.
Most of the main street, where my father had a store, and, of course, the neighborhood stores, also a little other, smaller streets, 99% of Jews had the stores.
So all the business was in Jewish hands.
It came Shabbos, there was no such a thing, one Jewish store should be open.
It happened once that a guy, who had a drug store, and who did-- who was not religious at all, and he came up with the idea to aggravate our rabbi, the Munkatsh rabbi.
And he had a big, big drug store.
And he opened at Shabbos.
Came one of the Hasidic Jew, broke this window, and never again before was a store open Shabbos.
How about for you and your family, what was Shabbos like in your house?
We were strictly Orthodox Jews.
We-- I went to a cheder before I start to go to Hebrew gymnasium.
My house was always kosher.
And we observed the Shabbos.
And there was no such a thing to be a Shabbos not to go to the shul.
After the shul, we used to go-- once, to my father's parents.
And there, he had lunch.
And once in a while, to my mother's parents.
But most of them, the family was nice and big.
As I'm going to show it, later, in the pictures.
The-- the family was always together.
The mostly, Shabbos meal was prepared as most by the Jewish, even here, in America.
There's a chicken soup.
First, of course, is the gefilte fish, then comes the chicken soup, with the-- with the kneidlach.
And of meat, of course, all kind of meat.
And then, after, all kind of sweet stuff.
Where I used to live, in Munkatsh they called it [HUNGARIAN].
It was a Hungarian name, because, at this time, we were already occupied by the Hungarian.
And my grandfather's, from the father's side, he was the founder of this shul.
It was a little shul.
But we had approximately, in the shul, I would say, about between 75 and 100 people every Shabbos.
From my mother's side, where the shul was two blocks away, it was a bigger shul.
There were already about 500, 600 Jews.
And this happened to be in [NON-ENGLISH] in Munkatsh.
Usually, I went to the small shul, with my father, should rest in peace.
And later on, once in a while, it was in a family, some wedding or bar mitzvah or some holiday or some special occasion or some rabbi came from another town, so we were invited to the other shul.
But most of the time, ours was with my father in the little shul, in my home town, where I used to live in the same street, in the same street was the shul.
There was a rabbi.
But if you ask me what was his name, I don't know.
To be honest, I was too young to understand the real antisemitism.
But once the start of the war, and the Hungarians occupied, roughly 1938, our hometown, we started to feel the antisemitism, day after day, more and more.
First of all, they looked for all those Jews who didn't have citizenship.
They came from other countries, mostly from Poland.
They were taken away, already, started in 1941, 1942.
Slowly, they took them away.
Of course, we Jews, we thought only they mean the Polish people.
We was never thinking that it would come the time they will take us, too.
And this was the plan.
It went from one country to another country.
They took all the Jews away to concentration camp.
During the Hungarian time, the Hungarians-- let me point it out-- they were bigger antisemites than even the Germans.
Any problem, what they could do for the Jews, they were first there.
First, they started to make laws.
In only certain schools could go Jewish people.
I went to the Hebrew gymnasium, so I didn't have this problem.
But then later on, they took away, from Jewish people, the right to have stores.
And more and more Jewish people lost their stores.
They couldn't make a living.
And they were in big, big problem.
It affected my father's store also, because, already in 1941 or '42, they took him to forced labor.
The Hungarians, they didn't the Jews in the army.
The Hungarians, they took the Jews, they put them, a yellow band on their wrist.
And they had to go clean the streets.
They'd send them to Russia to clean the mines.
They sent them to places where they never returned.
Of course, my mother took over, as I mentioned before, the store.
And she was a brave woman and a smart woman.
And she took care of us.
Yes, it happened once.
I used to live in a street, there was a mixed crowd, gentiles and Jews.
Of course, there was a Munkatsh section, where only strictly Jews used to be.
In fact, there was a street they called it, [HUNGARIAN].
It means Jewish street.
And this was around where the Munkatsh rabbi used to live and the big shul, that thousands of Jews attend there, where there was a-- a mikveh, a big one.
And there is a-- a big market, fish market.
This was a real, real Jewish neighborhood.
I used to live in a neighborhood, where it was a mixed.
Now, in the beginning, because I did not where the payos, like most of the family.
From my father's side and from mother's side, the children did it.
But I was a little more modern.
And probably, my father grow this, to this point, to be so modern.
And [? once, ?] [? we had ?] a case, when children used to play in the street, one of the guys, who was now a Jew, who was a gentile, he invited me to the basement, we should play together.
And we played together.
And he put out sandwiches.
In one of the sandwiches, I saw it was ham.
And he told me, take a sandwich.
And I said, I wouldn't eat this sandwich.
And he says, why?
I say, because this is ham, and I am Jewish.
And I am eating only kosher.
And he said, you are Jewish?
He looked at me like he didn't believe it, that I'm Jewish.
I said, no, I wouldn't eat it.
And he said, you know, you do-- you don't look like the rest of the people, of the rest up the Jews.
I said, what do you see on me more or different than between the rest of the Jews?
Of course, probably, he has this in mind, they have horns, and I haven't.
And the non-Jews, they haven't got horns.
That's the only thing for probably he was thinking in his mind.
But since this, then later on, I never played with them.
And I felt that I'm not invited anymore there.
And that's how it was, the situation between the Jews and non-Jews.
If a Jewish kid, on the street, was walking with their payos, many, many times, before he arrived back to his home, he was beaten from the goyishe kinder.
And it was not pleasant to be between the goyim.
Yes.
In fact, not only Hebrew gymnasium.
We started, in Munkatsh, it was in Russia, we called it Carpathian.
And in the whole area, this was the most educated school what could exist.
And I started, already, from kindergarten.
We has, already, Hebrew kindergarten, lower classes, and gymnasium.
In fact, we had also the girls attended to polgari.
Boys went to gymnasium and the girls, they went to polgari, till the fourth gymnasium.
Then at fourth gymnasium, they united the boys and the girls in gymnasium.
Hebrew gymnasium was in a high level.
And to explain it to you why, we had next to Munkatsh, another 40 kilometer, another town, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was also having a Hebrew gymnasium.
But I would say, from the whole study, of what they studied there, was altogether maybe 10% or 15% in Hebrew, the rest was in Hungarian.
In our Hebrew school, 90% you can say was in Hebrew.
Physic, we learned in Hebrew language, history in Hebrew language.
Math, we learned in Hebrew language.
So they were concentrated, really, to talk just Hebrew.
We Had in the school, in Hebrew gymnasium, uniform hats.
Each hat has an emblem, what-- like a Ten Commandments pin on it.
And we had also some rim.
It means guards.
If we were, in the morning, in the school, afternoon, they used to be on the street and watching the students, who is talking Hebrew?
We supposed to talk only Hebrew and not the Hungarian.
If any boy or girl, they catched who is not talking Hebrew, they just mark down their name.
And next day, we have to pay in a few pennies fine, because we didn't spoke Hebrew.
We spoke Hungarian.
So that's how we learned the language so good.
When I arrived, in Israel, in 1949, my Hebrew just as fluently as a Sabra who was born in Israel.
And what language did you speak in the home, amongst yourselves, with the family?
We started to speak in my parents' Jewish.
Then, later on, I spoke Hungarian, too.
Because that was the official language.
And I don't remember the Czechish language, because at this time, I didn't-- I was never in a Czechish school.
But mostly, in Munkatsh, in this town, the average public knew Czechish, Russia, Jewish.
Every child knew these three languages.
Yes, we had an hour.
It was Tanakh, we called it.
Yeah.
Of course, it was always observed, in Hebrew gymnasium, the Shabbos.
We was not allowed to-- it was not exactly a yeshiva, but it was a Hebrew school.
And we learned also about Jewish history.
You're talking about, between 1938 and 1944?
As the Hungarian occupied Hungary, the Jewish situation were, day by day, worse and worse.
The Hungarians are big antisemites.
The Hungarians, they hate Jews.
They were waiting for this minute, for Auschwitz, like we Jews are waiting for Messiah.
They were worse than the Germans.
They were hitting Jews for no reason whatsoever.
Wherever they could do trouble for Jews, it was done.
And as I mentioned before, day after day, they took away more, more stores, and mostly Jewish people there, business people.
Of course, if somebody was a doctor or somebody was a professional, he continued his profession.
But those business people, they were in a big, big problem.
If I'm not mistaken, it started toward 1941 or '42.
First he went for six months.
Later on, they went for eight months.
And by the end, they took all these people to Russia, to the front.
They had to help the Hungarian army to sweep the mines.
So they were just like-- they died so fast.
I would say, all these people, who left to Russia, I don't know if 10% came back-- not even 10%.
How was your father notified that he was going to have to leave?
They just came out with a law that, for this age up to this age, every Jewish man has to appear, city hall, register himself.
And they took him.
They didn't care if that guy had a family, how this guy will live, how the family will survive.
With my father, it was a [? lock, ?] because when they, each year, as he was in the forced labor, in 1944, they wanted to take him also to send him to Russia.
And he had trouble with his kidneys.
And he-- when they send him the letter to be-- go to the army, again, to the forced labor, he had a connection in Budapest.
And they took him in, in a hospital, with a little connection, with a certain doctor.
They called it [HUNGARIAN] Sanatorium.
And he was there about two weeks.
So when came the paper, he should go to the army, the doctor said, he has to operated.
He will stay here.
Of course, he was there two weeks in the hospital.
And the doctor said, Mr.
Schlesinger, we will do everything for you, what we can.
But there, you have to make up your mind, or you going to have the operation, or we have to send you out, and you have to go to the army, to the forced labor?
So the Munkatsh rebbe, who was very famous, a Munkatsh rebbe.
He had a son-in-law, who came from Poland, a well-educated man, who is still today in Israel.
He used to be the chief rabbi in South America, in Brazil.
And later on, he became, in Lehavot, also a chief rabbi.
He could be also in Tel Aviv a chief rabbi, but because of the Zionism, between the religious and non-religious, so probably that was the main reason.
That's the way I heard, he couldn't become the rabbi.
Now he was sent to Poland, with his family.
Of course, whoever was sent to Poland never came back.
But the Munkatsh rabbi had such a connection in city hall.
And of course, before the Hungarian came in, and even during the Hungarian, they gave him big respect, because he's a very educated, smart man.
And he had the connection to send a Hungarian officer, to Poland, to dress up his son-in-law for a Hungarian officer and to bring him back from Poland.
This particular rabbi, this son-in-law, as they brought him back, he knew he cannot exist anymore in Munkatsh.
And they send him up-- also, for a lot of money-- to Budapest, the capital town of Hungary.
And my father had a-- a chance to talk to the rabbi.
And he ask his advice, what would you suggest me?
Should I make the operation?
Or shall I go to the forced labor?
And this rabbi advised him, operation, we have always time.
To go for a knife, and this is not so sexful-- successful, those days, you know?
It was not so advanced, like today.
And he left the hospital.
And he took the chance, instead of to go under a knife, he will go to forced labor.
It was a miracle.
A few days later, it came out that this year's, or those people who were in this particular year born, they don't have to go to this forced labor.
That's how he survived.
So we went to Auschwitz with my father, with my mother, and the whole family, from both sides, from my mother's side and from my father's side.
So this last time, he did-- your father did not have to go?
No, no, because it came out, a law, that this particular year is not going to forced labor.
You talking about in forced labor?
Yeah.
It was in-- in Hungary.
It was not far from Munkatsh, sometimes 50 kilometers, sometimes 60 kilometers.
In fact, sometimes, the whole family went to this little village where he used to serve there.
And we used to see him, the weekends.
And sometimes, he used to pay for the officers to let him out for a few days.
It happened once to him.
He paid for the office to let him out.
And somebody made a complaint, that Schlesinger left.
And it looks like it was not exactly so official, his leaving to a vacation.
And then he came back, Sunday, they gave him a punishment.
They put him out on a string, had to hang him.
And a few times, he fainted.
Almost, he died.
But they saved him.
So it wasn't so pleasant, actually, the vacation.
But he survived.
My father was a strong man.
And we always had a nice life.
So even if you had to suffer a little bit, I didn't see him that he should look so bad.
But other people, who came from families, where they were not rich, and where they didn't have extra food from home, they were suffering.
As much as she could, yeah.
Yeah.
In this respect, we had no problem, those days, until he was there in this labor camp.
OK.
